title: Common Git Commands
description: Daily utilities for checking out branches, staging changes, staging logs, and publishing revisions.
codeBlock:
# Stage changes and commit
git add .
git commit -m "Update docs"

# Push changes to origin branch
git push origin main
details:
Git is a distributed version control system that models project history as a directed acyclic graph of commits, each referencing a complete snapshot of the working tree rather than a set of diffs. This content-addressable architecture — where every object (blob, tree, commit, tag) is identified by its SHA-1 hash — provides cryptographic integrity verification and enables efficient branching and merging. Unlike centralized systems such as Subversion, every Git clone is a full-fledged repository with complete history, enabling offline work and decentralized collaboration across distributed teams.

The staging area (index) is one of Git's most distinctive features: it allows users to compose commits incrementally by selectively staging changes from the working directory. Combined with interactive rebase and the granular control of git add -p, this architecture supports clean, atomic commit histories. The three-tree model (working directory → index → HEAD) forms the core of Git's internal model, as documented in Pro Git by Scott Chacon and Ben Straub, the canonical reference on Git internals and workflows.

Effective Git usage extends beyond memorizing commands to understanding the underlying object model. Concepts like detached HEAD states, the reflog for recovery, and the distinction between fast-forward and three-way merges become intuitive when viewed through the lens of the commit DAG. For teams, establishing a consistent branching strategy — whether Git Flow, GitHub Flow, or trunk-based development — is essential for maintaining a coherent and navigable project history. The official Git documentation remains the definitive reference for command semantics, edge cases, and configuration options.
